items.q: What does the 'tragedy of the commons' describe?
items.correct: Shared resources tend to be overexploited because individuals benefit personally while sharing costs.
items.misconception: It is a play about poor people.
items.why: Students take 'commons' as a place. Hardin's metaphor describes a STRUCTURAL PROBLEM: rational individual choices lead to collective ruin when costs are externalised - applies to overfishing, climate, and groundwater.

items.q: Why is atmospheric nitrogen unavailable to most plants directly?
items.correct: N2 has a strong triple bond that most organisms cannot break.
items.misconception: Because plants cannot reach high enough to access it.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Despite 78% of air being N2, the molecule's triple bond is energetically costly to break. Only specialised nitrogen-fixing bacteria (and lightning, industrial Haber process) can convert it to usable forms.

items.correct: Water vapour released from plant leaves to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: Water flowing through soil into rivers.
items.why: Students confuse transpiration with other water movements. It's specifically water LOSS THROUGH PLANTS - significant in forested areas, contributing substantially to atmospheric water vapour over land.
Correct answer: Carbon dioxide. “Oxygen” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Trap heat. “Cool Earth” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: By roughly how many degrees C has Earth warmed since 1900?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
